title: Circulating Nucleic Acids as Liquid Biopsies and Noninvasive Disease B
section: Genetics, Aging, and Frontiers in Medicine
authors: Ash A. Alizadeh, Kiran K. Khush, Yair J. Blumenfeld

items.subtopic: Circulating Nucleic Acids as Liquid Biopsies and Noninvasive Disease Biomarkers | CELL-FREE DNA IN ONCOLOGY
items.title: The Role of Cell-Free DNA (cfDNA) in Oncology for Less Invasive Cancer Monitoring
items.content: Cell-free DNA (cfDNA), a subset of circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA), offers a less invasive alternative to traditional tumor biopsies by allowing cancer monitoring and molecular profiling through blood samples. This approach addresses the limitations of tumor heterogeneity and the risks associated with direct tissue sampling.
keyPoints: Tumor-derived cfDNA was first observed in plasma over four decades after its initial description in 1948.
cfDNA is fragmented, correlating with DNA wrapped around nucleosomes, complicating differentiation from normal DNA.
Next-generation sequencing methods enhance sensitivity for identifying and quantifying ctDNA amidst a background of normal DNA.

items.subtopic: Circulating Nucleic Acids as Liquid Biopsies and Noninvasive Disease Biomarkers | CELL-FREE DNA IN ONCOLOGY
items.title: Applications of Circulating Tumor DNA (ctDNA) in Cancer Management
items.content: Circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) serves multiple roles in oncology, including early cancer detection, noninvasive genotyping and classification, monitoring molecular response during therapy, and assessing minimal residual disease post-therapy.
keyPoints: ctDNA is used for studying tumor biology and detecting cancers through blood plasma analysis.
Next-generation sequencing aids in distinguishing ctDNA from normal DNA to facilitate precision medicine.

items.subtopic: Circulating Nucleic Acids as Liquid Biopsies and Noninvasive Disease Biomarkers | CELL-FREE DNA IN ONCOLOGY
items.title: Challenges and Advancements in cfDNA Analysis for Oncology
items.content: While cfDNA analysis offers a promising non-invasive method for cancer detection, challenges include the need to differentiate ctDNA from normal DNA due to its fragmented nature. Advances in next-generation sequencing have improved sensitivity and specificity in identifying tumor-derived cfDNA.
keyPoints: cfDNA is naturally fragmented with lengths correlating to nucleosome-wrapped DNA.
The major challenge lies in achieving sufficient sensitivity to identify ctDNA against a background of normal DNA.

items.subtopic: EARLY CANCER DETECTION
items.title: Challenges and Potential of ctDNA in Early Cancer Detection
items.content: Current cancer screening methods face limitations such as high false discovery rates, low compliance, and lack of integration with molecular profiling insights. Circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) analysis offers a promising approach for blood-based early detection by leveraging mutations, methylation patterns, and other biomarkers.
keyPoints: Annual radiologic screening for lung cancer in high-risk populations has a ~90% false discovery rate.
No randomized studies to date have demonstrated survival advantage using liquid biopsies with clinical endpoints.
Future studies are needed to establish sensitivity and specificity of multicancer early detection (MCED) tests.
items.reference: Circulating Nucleic Acids as Liquid Biopsies and Noninvasive Disease B · EARLY CANCER DETECTION

items.subtopic: EARLY CANCER DETECTION
items.title: Need for Prospective Studies in Liquid Biopsy Screening
items.content: While ctDNA analysis shows promise, its clinical adoption is hindered by the lack of evidence from randomized trials showing improved survival outcomes. Future studies should focus on long-term follow-up to assess the true efficacy and accuracy of MCED tests.
keyPoints: No prospective studies have applied liquid biopsies in randomized trials with survival outcomes as endpoints.
Studies are required to determine if early detection improves cancer-specific survival or reduces false positives.
False-positive results from current methods can lead to costly secondary invasive procedures.
items.reference: Circulating Nucleic Acids as Liquid Biopsies and Noninvasive Disease B · EARLY CANCER DETECTION

items.subtopic: NONINVASIVE TUMOR GENOTYPING
items.title: Utility of Liquid Biopsies in Advanced Malignancies for Targeted Therapy
items.content: Liquid biopsies provide a noninvasive method to determine tumor genotype, which is crucial for selecting targeted therapies. They have high positive predictive value and FDA-approved assays can identify mutations associated with responses to specific treatments across various cancers.
keyPoints: Targetable mutations in EGFR are common in NSCLC and correlate with response to TKIs.
Liquid biopsies like Guardant360 and FoundationOne Liquid CDx target >50 genes for noninvasive genotyping.
FDA-approved assays identify mutations linked to therapies such as osimertinib, amivantamab, sotorasib, racaparib, alectinib, alpelisib, and olaparib.
Liquid biopsies are less reliable for early-stage tumors due to low circulating variant allele fractions (<0.5%).
In advanced cancers with low metastatic burden, tumor biopsy may still be needed if mutations are not detected in blood.
items.reference: Circulating Nucleic Acids as Liquid Biopsies and Noninvasive Disease B · NONINVASIVE TUMOR GENOTYPING

items.subtopic: NONINVASIVE TUMOR GENOTYPING
items.title: Limitations of Liquid Biopsies for Minimal Residual Disease (MRD) Detection
items.content: While liquid biopsies are effective for genotyping in advanced malignancies, they have limitations in detecting minimal residual disease due to low sensitivity and circulating variant allele fractions typically below 0.5%.
keyPoints: Liquid biopsy assays are not optimal for MRD detection post-treatment of localized cancers.
SEQC2 Project found ctDNA detection less reliable below a circulating variant allele fraction of 0.5%.
Residual ctDNA allele fractions after treatment are usually much lower than 0.5%.
In cases of low metastatic tumor burden, additional tumor biopsy may be necessary to confirm mutations for therapy selection.
items.reference: Circulating Nucleic Acids as Liquid Biopsies and Noninvasive Disease B · NONINVASIVE TUMOR GENOTYPING

items.subtopic: BIOLOGIC CONSIDERATIONS
items.title: Understanding Clonal Hematopoiesis and Its Impact on Liquid Biopsies
items.content: Clonal hematopoiesis (CH) arises from age-related mutations in hematopoietic stem cells, contributing to biologic background noise in liquid biopsies. This can lead to false-positive results when detecting circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA). The prevalence of CH variants increases with age and is particularly significant in adults over 60 years old.
keyPoints: CH mutations arise from hematopoietic stem cells due to age-related clonal hematopoiesis.
Identification of mutations in ~20 genes exceeding 2% allelic fraction defines clonal hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential (CHIP).
Prevalence of CH variants approaches 100% in adults >60 years old.
CH is a major source of false-positive findings in liquid biopsies, especially affecting genes like DNMT3A, TET2, ASXL1, and JAK2.
15–41% of cfDNA mutations in the TP53 gene can be attributable to CH.
items.reference: Circulating Nucleic Acids as Liquid Biopsies and Noninvasive Disease B · BIOLOGIC CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: BIOLOGIC CONSIDERATIONS
items.title: Mitigating False Positives in Liquid Biopsies Due to Nonmalignant Sources
items.content: To avoid false-positive results from cfDNA mutations arising from clonal hematopoiesis (CH) or other noncirculating sources, direct genotyping of peripheral blood leukocytes is recommended. This helps distinguish true ctDNA from background noise caused by CH in nonhematopoietic tissues.
keyPoints: ~10% of cfDNA mutations in healthy adults may not be found in matched leukocytes, indicating other sources such as bone marrow or nonmalignant cell types.
Recurrent somatic mutations can occur in genes like KRAS, MED12, and BRAF within nonmalignant proliferations.
Sequencing tumor tissue initially helps identify true somatic mutations for monitoring in plasma samples.
items.reference: Circulating Nucleic Acids as Liquid Biopsies and Noninvasive Disease B · BIOLOGIC CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: PRETREATMENT TUMOR BURDEN | MONITORING RESPONSE TO TREATMENT
items.title: Pretreatment ctDNA Levels as Prognostic Indicators in Cancer
items.content: Pretreatment circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) levels can serve as a noninvasive prognostic tool for assessing tumor burden and predicting treatment outcomes. These levels correlate with established measures of tumor burden such as stage, metabolic tumor volume, and specific serum protein markers like lactate dehydrogenase in lymphomas, CA19-9 in pancreatic cancers, and carcinoembryonic antigen in colorectal cancers.
keyPoints: Pretreatment ctDNA levels are associated with established measures of tumor burden (stage, metabolic tumor volume, serum protein markers).
Strong prognostic value for treatment failure and disease progression.
Can be used to measure tumor burden poorly captured by other indices, reducing biases in clinical trials.
Example thresholds: 100-fold reduction in ctDNA after one cycle of induction chemotherapy predicts early molecular response; 2.5-log reductions predict major molecular response.
items.reference: Circulating Nucleic Acids as Liquid Biopsies and Noninvasive Disease B · PRETREATMENT TUMOR BURDEN | MONITORING RESPONSE TO TREATMENT

items.subtopic: PRETREATMENT TUMOR BURDEN | MONITORING RESPONSE TO TREATMENT
items.title: Using Liquid Biopsies to Monitor Treatment Response in Cancer
items.content: Liquid biopsies, particularly ctDNA levels, are valuable for monitoring therapeutic responses in cancer. They provide quantitative and qualitative insights into treatment efficacy, similar to functional imaging in systemic response assessment. Key factors include tumor histology, treatment setting, type of therapy, timing of ctDNA assessments, assay sensitivity/specificity, and defined thresholds for ctDNA level changes.
keyPoints: Functional imaging is used alongside liquid biopsies for monitoring therapeutic responses.
Quantitative changes in ctDNA levels can indicate early molecular response.
100-fold (2-log) reduction in ctDNA after one cycle of chemotherapy indicates an early molecular response.
2.5-log reductions in ctDNA after two cycles define a major molecular response, associated with improved survival outcomes.
items.reference: Circulating Nucleic Acids as Liquid Biopsies and Noninvasive Disease B · PRETREATMENT TUMOR BURDEN | MONITORING RESPONSE TO TREATMENT

items.subtopic: MINIMAL RESIDUAL DISEASE
items.title: Predictive Value of ctDNA MRD in Relapse Risk Assessment
items.content: Liquid biopsies, including circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA), have demonstrated high positive predictive value for predicting relapse risk across various cancer types such as lung, colon, rectum, bladder, and breast cancers. This is particularly relevant when considering adjuvant therapies post-surgery.
keyPoints: Liquid biopsies can detect minimal residual disease (MRD) in solid tumors.
High positive predictive value for predicting relapse risk using ctDNA-based techniques.
Clinical utility of checkpoint inhibitor atezolizumab is likely limited to patients with detectable ctDNA MRD post-surgery.
items.reference: Circulating Nucleic Acids as Liquid Biopsies and Noninvasive Disease B · MINIMAL RESIDUAL DISEASE

items.subtopic: MINIMAL RESIDUAL DISEASE
items.title: Challenges and Considerations in ctDNA MRD Detection for Treatment Personalization
items.content: Despite the promising predictive value of ctDNA MRD, current assays have modest clinical sensitivity, leading to false negatives. Improvements are needed in assay performance to effectively use liquid biopsies for guiding adjuvant therapies.
keyPoints: Clinical sensitivity of current ctDNA MRD assays is generally modest.
False negatives can lead to relapses in patients classified as MRD negative.
Optimal use requires improvements in analytical and clinical sensitivity.
items.reference: Circulating Nucleic Acids as Liquid Biopsies and Noninvasive Disease B · MINIMAL RESIDUAL DISEASE

items.subtopic: TECHNICAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.title: Analytical Sensitivity of Liquid Biopsies
items.content: Recent advancements in molecular techniques and computational methods have significantly improved the analytical sensitivity of liquid biopsies, enabling the detection of tumor-derived circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) at very low levels.
keyPoints: Liquid biopsies can identify ctDNA through amplification and detection techniques such as PCR and high-throughput sequencing.
Analytical sensitivities in parts-per-million range are achievable with multiplexed sequencing-based strategies, especially when leveraging tumor genotypes.
items.reference: Circulating Nucleic Acids as Liquid Biopsies and Noninvasive Disease B · TECHNICAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: TECHNICAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.title: Tumor Genotype-Informed Liquid Biopsy Approach
items.content: Incorporating tumor genotype information from tissue profiling into liquid biopsy analysis enhances sensitivity and specificity by reducing false positives and the volume of blood samples required.
keyPoints: Tumor genotype-informed approaches track mutations identified in tumor tissues within posttreatment plasma, improving assay performance.
This method reduces background error sources and is less demanding on blood sample volumes.
items.reference: Circulating Nucleic Acids as Liquid Biopsies and Noninvasive Disease B · TECHNICAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: CELL-FREE DNA IN TRANSPLANTATION | NONINVASIVE DETECTION OF ACUTE ALLOGRAFT REJECTION (part 1)
items.title: Role of dd-cfDNA in Detecting Acute Allograft Rejection
items.content: Donor-derived cell-free DNA (dd-cfDNA) levels increase significantly during acute allograft rejection and can be used to noninvasively monitor transplant recipients. This biomarker correlates with the severity of rejection, allowing for early detection and intervention.
keyPoints: Elevated dd-cfDNA indicates graft injury due to acute rejection.
Threshold value of 0.25% has a 99% negative predictive value for acute rejection in heart transplants.
dd-cfDNA levels rise before clinical signs, enabling early intervention.
items.reference: Circulating Nucleic Acids as Liquid Biopsies and Noninvasive Disease B · CELL-FREE DNA IN TRANSPLANTATION | NONINVASIVE DETECTION OF ACUTE ALLOGRAFT REJECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: CELL-FREE DNA IN TRANSPLANTATION | NONINVASIVE DETECTION OF ACUTE ALLOGRAFT REJECTION (part 1)
items.title: Differentiating Rejection Types with dd-cfDNA
items.content: dd-cfDNA patterns, including levels and fragment types, can help differentiate between antibody-mediated and cellular rejection in transplant recipients.
keyPoints: Higher dd-cfDNA levels are associated with antibody-mediated rejection compared to cellular rejection.
Different cfDNA fragment types and lengths indicate different rejection subtypes.
items.reference: Circulating Nucleic Acids as Liquid Biopsies and Noninvasive Disease B · CELL-FREE DNA IN TRANSPLANTATION | NONINVASIVE DETECTION OF ACUTE ALLOGRAFT REJECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: CELL-FREE DNA IN TRANSPLANTATION | NONINVASIVE DETECTION OF ACUTE ALLOGRAFT REJECTION (part 1)
items.title: Utility of dd-cfDNA in Kidney Transplantation
items.content: In kidney transplantation, elevated dd-cfDNA levels correlate with acute rejection and long-term graft outcomes. However, they can also indicate other pathologies like pyelonephritis.
keyPoints: A dd-cfDNA cutoff of ≥1.0% has an AUC of 0.74 for detecting acute rejection in kidney transplants.
Elevated levels (≥0.5%) correlate with clinical and subclinical rejection, affecting graft survival.
dd-cfDNA is more indicative of severe graft injury rather than rejection alone.
items.reference: Circulating Nucleic Acids as Liquid Biopsies and Noninvasive Disease B · CELL-FREE DNA IN TRANSPLANTATION | NONINVASIVE DETECTION OF ACUTE ALLOGRAFT REJECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: NONINVASIVE DETECTION OF ACUTE ALLOGRAFT REJECTION (part 2)
items.title: Role of dd-cfDNA in Detecting and Managing Acute Allograft Rejection
items.content: Donor-derived cell-free DNA (dd-cfDNA) levels are elevated during acute rejection episodes post-transplantation, correlating with the severity of rejection. These levels provide prognostic information even without overt allograft injury, predicting long-term outcomes such as chronic dysfunction and graft failure.
keyPoints: Elevated dd-cfDNA levels detected weeks before biopsy in lung transplantation indicate acute rejection.
Higher dd-cfDNA levels correlate with severe rejection and antibody-mediated rejection compared to T-cell-mediated rejection.
High average dd-cfDNA within the first 3 months post-lung transplant predicts chronic allograft dysfunction and failure.
In kidney transplants, high dd-cfDNA at discharge predicts graft dysfunction at 1 year.
dd-cfDNA levels reflect cumulative graft injury and long-term adverse outcomes.
items.reference: Circulating Nucleic Acids as Liquid Biopsies and Noninvasive Disease B · NONINVASIVE DETECTION OF ACUTE ALLOGRAFT REJECTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: NONINVASIVE DETECTION OF ACUTE ALLOGRAFT REJECTION (part 2)
items.title: Clinical Utility of dd-cfDNA Testing in Transplantation
items.content: dd-cfDNA testing, using assays like AlloSure and Prospera, offers a noninvasive method to monitor graft health. While it cannot replace biopsies entirely, it reduces the need for them by identifying patients who truly require biopsy, thus improving safety and cost-effectiveness.
keyPoints: dd-cfDNA can be detected in plasma post-transplantation, with levels initially high then stabilizing at low baseline unless injury occurs.
Assays like AlloSure and Prospera use SNP panels to differentiate donor from recipient cfDNA without requiring genotyping.
These assays are rapid, cost-effective compared to whole genome sequencing, and available for clinical use.
dd-cfDNA levels rise with acute rejection and fall after successful treatment, aiding in personalized immunosuppressive management.
items.reference: Circulating Nucleic Acids as Liquid Biopsies and Noninvasive Disease B · NONINVASIVE DETECTION OF ACUTE ALLOGRAFT REJECTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: MONITORING MICROBIAL DIVERSITY AND INFECTION AFTER TRANSPLANTATION
items.title: Use cfDNA Sequencing for Noninvasive Diagnosis of Infections Post-Transplantation
items.content: Hypothesis-free sequencing of circulating cell-free DNA (cfDNA) in transplant recipients can provide insights into microbial diversity and potential infections. This method allows for the detection of viral, bacterial, and fungal genomes without prior assumptions about the source of infection.
keyPoints: 2% of cfDNA sequences are microbial in origin, including viruses (73%), bacteria (25%), and fungi (2%).
Viral counts from sequencing correlate with quantitative PCR assays for common posttransplant viral pathogens like herpesviruses.
cfDNA sequencing can identify infections such as cytomegalovirus, adenovirus, polyomavirus, herpesvirus, and microsporidia that may be missed by traditional methods (AUC 0.91 for CMV).
The virome composition changes with immunosuppressive and antiviral therapies, aiding in monitoring treatment effects.
items.reference: Circulating Nucleic Acids as Liquid Biopsies and Noninvasive Disease B · MONITORING MICROBIAL DIVERSITY AND INFECTION AFTER TRANSPLANTATION

items.subtopic: MONITORING MICROBIAL DIVERSITY AND INFECTION AFTER TRANSPLANTATION
items.title: Impact of Immunosuppressive Therapy on Microbial Composition Post-Transplantation
items.content: Immunosuppressive drugs significantly influence the microbial composition in transplant recipients, with high doses leading to a virome dominated by Anelloviridae. Monitoring these changes can help manage and adjust therapeutic strategies.
keyPoints: High levels of immunosuppressive drugs result in a virome dominated by Anelloviridae or torque teno viruses.
Herpesvirus levels decrease with the introduction of antiviral prophylaxis like valganciclovir.
Quantitative agreement exists between viral counts from sequencing and PCR assays for posttransplant pathogens.
Establishment of pathogen-specific thresholds is necessary to differentiate colonization, infection, and disease.
items.reference: Circulating Nucleic Acids as Liquid Biopsies and Noninvasive Disease B · MONITORING MICROBIAL DIVERSITY AND INFECTION AFTER TRANSPLANTATION

items.subtopic: MONITORING HOST IMMUNITY
items.title: Anellovirus cfDNA as a Marker of Immunosuppression in Transplant Recipients
items.content: The relative abundance of anellovirus circulating cell-free DNA (cfDNA) is highly sensitive to changes in immunosuppression levels, making it a potential marker for monitoring the immune status in transplant recipients. An increase in anellovirus cfDNA correlates with higher degrees of immunosuppression.
keyPoints: Anelloviridae family accounts for 68% of virome in lung transplant recipients by plasma cfDNA sequencing.
Relative abundance of anellovirus cfDNA ranges from <5% pre-transplant to 84% during months 4.5–6 post-transplant, when immunosuppression is most profound.

An increase in anellovirus load correlates with lower risk of acute rejection (HR = 0.48) and higher risk of infectious complications (HR = 5.05) per $ \log_{10} $ increase in lung transplant recipients.
items.reference: Circulating Nucleic Acids as Liquid Biopsies and Noninvasive Disease B · MONITORING HOST IMMUNITY

items.subtopic: MONITORING HOST IMMUNITY
items.title: Clinical Applications of cfDNA Sequencing Post-Transplantation
items.content: Circulating cell-free DNA (cfDNA) sequencing offers multiple clinical applications post-transplant, including monitoring for acute rejection and assessing the microbiome. It provides early detection of allograft injury and can guide individualized immunosuppressive therapy.
keyPoints: Donor-derived cfDNA assays are used for noninvasive surveillance in heart, lung, and kidney transplantation.
cfDNA sequencing allows monitoring changes in the microbiome post-transplantation due to immunosuppression and antimicrobial therapy adjustments.
Anellovirus cfDNA quantification can assess host immune response status, aiding in individualized immunosuppressive treatment.
items.reference: Circulating Nucleic Acids as Liquid Biopsies and Noninvasive Disease B · MONITORING HOST IMMUNITY

items.subtopic: CELL-FREE DNA IN PRENATAL MEDICINE
items.title: Noninvasive Prenatal Testing with cfDNA for Fetal Aneuploidy
items.content: Cell-free fetal DNA (cfDNA) technology offers a noninvasive alternative to traditional invasive prenatal diagnostic tests like amniocentesis and chorionic villus sampling, which carry risks such as miscarriage. cfDNA testing provides higher accuracy in detecting fetal aneuploidies compared to traditional screening methods that combine maternal serum analyte levels with first-trimester sonographic assessment.
keyPoints: cfDNA tests are noninvasive and safer than amniocentesis or chorionic villus sampling, which carry risks of miscarriage, preterm rupture of membranes, and preterm birth.
Traditional prenatal screening methods have lower detection rates and higher false-positive rates compared to cfDNA testing.
cfDNA technology has advanced beyond aneuploidy screening to include microdeletion/duplication syndromes and single-gene disorders.
items.reference: Circulating Nucleic Acids as Liquid Biopsies and Noninvasive Disease B · CELL-FREE DNA IN PRENATAL MEDICINE

items.subtopic: CELL-FREE DNA IN PRENATAL MEDICINE
items.title: Expanding Applications of cfDNA in Prenatal Medicine
items.content: Beyond fetal aneuploidy, cell-free fetal DNA (cfDNA) testing is now used to screen for microdeletion and duplication syndromes as well as single-gene disorders. Additionally, research into cell-free RNA in maternal serum aims to predict adverse pregnancy outcomes such as preterm birth and preeclampsia.
keyPoints: cfDNA tests have expanded from aneuploidy screening to include microdeletion/duplication syndromes and single-gene disorders.
Cell-free RNA in maternal serum is being explored for predicting adverse pregnancy outcomes like preterm birth and preeclampsia.
items.reference: Circulating Nucleic Acids as Liquid Biopsies and Noninvasive Disease B · CELL-FREE DNA IN PRENATAL MEDICINE

items.subtopic: NONINVASIVE PRENATAL TESTING FOR FETAL ANEUPLOIDY
items.title: Why cfDNA is preferred over fetal cells for noninvasive prenatal testing?
items.content: Cell-free DNA (cfDNA) in maternal blood offers several advantages over circulating fetal cells for detecting fetal aneuploidy. These include a higher fraction of fetal DNA (~5–20%) compared to the very small number of fetal cells, and the rapid decline of cfDNA levels post-delivery, which minimizes confusion with previous pregnancies.
keyPoints: Fetal cells are outnumbered by maternal red blood cells in the bloodstream.
The fraction of fetal DNA is significantly higher (~5–20%) than that of fetal cells.
cfDNA levels decline quickly after delivery, reducing risk from prior pregnancies.
items.reference: Circulating Nucleic Acids as Liquid Biopsies and Noninvasive Disease B · NONINVASIVE PRENATAL TESTING FOR FETAL ANEUPLOIDY

items.subtopic: NONINVASIVE PRENATIVE TESTING FOR FETAL ANEUPLOIDY
items.title: How does massive parallel sequencing aid in detecting fetal aneuploidy?
items.content: Massive parallel sequencing technology allows for the quantification of cfDNA by comparing sequencing reads from specific chromosomes against expected reference numbers. An excess of sequences from chromosome 21, for example, suggests trisomy 21 (Down's syndrome).
keyPoints: Sequencing compares reads from chromosome 21 to a reference number.
Excess sequences indicate potential trisomy 21.
Technique validated in landmark studies by Chiu and Fan in 2008.
items.reference: Circulating Nucleic Acids as Liquid Biopsies and Noninvasive Disease B · NONINVASIVE PRENATAL TESTING FOR FETAL ANEUPLOIDY

items.subtopic: NONINVASIVE PRENATIVE TESTING FOR FETAL ANEUPLOIDY
items.title: What are the limitations of cfDNA testing for fetal aneuploidy?
items.content: While cfDNA testing is highly sensitive (>99%) for trisomy 21, its sensitivity is lower for trisomies 18 and 13. The positive predictive value varies by chromosome and maternal age. Due to these factors, confirmatory diagnostic tests like amniocentesis or CVS are recommended following a positive cfDNA test.
keyPoints: Sensitivity >99% for trisomy 21; lower for trisomies 18 and 13.
Positive predictive value varies by chromosome and maternal age.
Confirmatory testing with amniocentesis or CVS is recommended.
items.reference: Circulating Nucleic Acids as Liquid Biopsies and Noninvasive Disease B · NONINVASIVE PRENATAL TESTING FOR FETAL ANEUPLOIDY

items.subtopic: NONINVASIVE PRENATAL TESTING FOR SEX CHROMOSOME ANEUPLOIDY AND RARE AUTOSOMAL TRISOMY
items.title: NIPT Accuracy for Sex Chromosome Aneuploidies and RAT Reporting
items.content: The accuracy of noninvasive prenatal testing (NIPT) is lower for sex chromosome aneuploidies compared to common autosomal aneuploidies due to differences in mosaicism among the fetus, placenta, and mother. Rare autosomal trisomies occur in less than 1% of pregnancies and are often associated with spontaneous miscarriage or adverse outcomes like fetal growth restriction; however, mosaic forms can result in normal outcomes. There is no consensus on whether NIPT should report RAT due to these variable implications.
keyPoints: NIPT accuracy for sex chromosome aneuploidies (e.g., 45,X, 47,XXY, 47,XXX, and 47,XY) is lower than for common autosomal aneuploidies.
Rare autosomal trisomy occurs in <1% of all pregnancies.
Mosaic RAT can be associated with both adverse outcomes and normal pregnancy results.
No universal recommendation exists on whether NIPT should report RAT.
items.reference: Circulating Nucleic Acids as Liquid Biopsies and Noninvasive Disease B · NONINVASIVE PRENATAL TESTING FOR SEX CHROMOSOME ANEUPLOIDY AND RARE AUTOSOMAL TRISOMY | DEPENDENCE ON FETAL FRACTION

items.subtopic: DEPENDENCE ON FETAL FRACTION
items.title: Impact of Fetal Fraction on NIPT Accuracy
items.content: The accuracy of NIPT is highly dependent on the fetal fraction in maternal serum, which increases with gestational age. Testing is recommended after 10 weeks' gestation to ensure adequate fetal DNA levels. Factors such as early gestational age (<10 weeks), maternal obesity, autoimmune disease, malignancy, and vanishing twins can lead to a 'no call' result due to low fetal fraction. A no-call result may be associated with increased aneuploidy risk, leading to controversy in management between repeat NIPT or invasive diagnostic testing.
keyPoints: NIPT accuracy depends on the fetal fraction in maternal serum.
Fetal DNA is present from 4–5 weeks' gestation but increases with age; testing recommended after 10 weeks.
<10 weeks, maternal obesity, autoimmune disease, malignancy, and vanishing twins can lead to 'no call' results.
No-call result may indicate increased aneuploidy risk; management options include repeat NIPT or invasive testing.
items.reference: Circulating Nucleic Acids as Liquid Biopsies and Noninvasive Disease B · NONINVASIVE PRENATAL TESTING FOR SEX CHROMOSOME ANEUPLOIDY AND RARE AUTOSOMAL TRISOMY | DEPENDENCE ON FETAL FRACTION

items.subtopic: NIPT IN MULTIPLE GESTATIONS
items.title: Utility of NIPT in Multiple Gestations
items.content: Noninvasive prenatal testing (NIPT) has evolved to effectively detect aneuploidies in multiple gestations, with detection rates comparable to singletons. Zygosity determination is crucial for accurate interpretation and management, especially in monozygotic twins where both share the same genetic makeup. Monochorionic twins are at higher risk than dichorionic ones.
keyPoints: NIPT aneuploidy detection rate in twin gestations mirrors that of singletons.
Zygosity testing is important for accurate NIPT interpretation, especially when placental number is unclear.
Monochorionic twins are higher risk than dichorionic twins and require different management.
items.reference: Circulating Nucleic Acids as Liquid Biopsies and Noninvasive Disease B · NIPT IN MULTIPLE GESTATIONS | NIPT FOR FETAL MICRODELETION/DUPLICATION SYNDROMES

items.subtopic: NIPT FOR FETAL MICRODELETION/DUPLICATION SYNDROMES
items.title: Limitations of NIPT in Detecting Microdeletion/Duplication Syndromes
items.content: While NIPT is useful for detecting fetal aneuploidies, its ability to detect microdeletion/duplication syndromes like 22q11 microdeletion or DiGeorges syndrome is less robust. Chromosomal microarray (CMA) remains the preferred method due to its higher resolution and accuracy in identifying copy number variants.
keyPoints: NIPT's detection rates for microdeletions/duplications are not as high as for aneuploidies.
Positive predictive values for CNVs detected by NIPT are lower.
CMA is preferred over traditional karyotype due to its ability to detect smaller genomic aberrations, with a resolution of ~5–10 MB.
items.reference: Circulating Nucleic Acids as Liquid Biopsies and Noninvasive Disease B · NIPT IN MULTIPLE GESTATIONS | NIPT FOR FETAL MICRODELETION/DUPLICATION SYNDROMES

items.subtopic: WHOLE GENOME NIPT AND SINGLE-GENE DISORDERS
items.title: Whole Genome NIPT: Considerations and Controversies
items.content: Next-generation sequencing in NIPT covers the entire genome but reports only a fraction of sequences. This raises debates about reporting whole genome results, especially when considering false-positive rates and abnormalities of unclear significance. The decision to limit results to known disease-causing mutations is also controversial.
keyPoints: NIPT platforms use next-generation sequencing for whole genome coverage.
Debate exists on whether to offer and report whole genome NIPT.
Concerns include false-positive rates and abnormalities of unclear significance.
Controversy over limiting results to known disease-causing mutations.
items.reference: Circulating Nucleic Acids as Liquid Biopsies and Noninvasive Disease B · WHOLE GENOME NIPT AND SINGLE-GENE DISORDERS | NIPT USING CELL-FREE RNA

items.subtopic: NIPT USING CELL-FREE RNA
items.title: Cell-Free Fetal RNA: Early Detection of Obstetric Complications
items.content: Cell-free fetal RNA in maternal serum provides insights into gene transcription and tissue-specific genes, offering a window into developing fetal organs. Serial analysis of cfRNA can detect changes as early as the first trimester, potentially identifying high-risk pregnancies months before clinical symptoms appear.
keyPoints: Cell-free fetal RNA offers a snapshot of active gene transcription during pregnancy.
Linked to tissue-specific genes, providing insights into fetal organ development.
Studied for early detection of preeclampsia, fetal growth restriction, and preterm birth.
Changes in circulating cfRNA can be detected from the first trimester.
items.reference: Circulating Nucleic Acids as Liquid Biopsies and Noninvasive Disease B · WHOLE GENOME NIPT AND SINGLE-GENE DISORDERS | NIPT USING CELL-FREE RNA

items.subtopic: NIPT DETECTING MATERNAL MALIGNANCY | BEYOND NEXT-GENERATION SEQUENCING | SUMMARY
items.title: Maternal malignancy detection via NIPT
items.content: Noninvasive prenatal testing (NIPT) can incidentally detect maternal malignancies due to its ability to sequence both fetal and maternal DNA. This is particularly relevant when uninterpretable results are obtained, which may be linked to undiagnosed maternal cancers.
keyPoints: Maternal malignancy complicates 1 in 1000 to 1 in 1500 pregnancies.
Common cancers include hematologic, breast, and gynecologic cancers (cervix and ovary).
NIPT can detect multiple aneuploidies or autosomal monosomes associated with occult maternal malignancies.
Incidental detection of maternal lymphoma, leukemia, gastrointestinal malignancy, and neuroendocrine tumors has been reported.

items.title: Advancements in fetal cell analysis for NIPT
items.content: Recent technological advances have revived the potential of isolating circulating fetal cells, allowing for whole-gene genome amplification and detailed genetic profiling. This could enhance early detection capabilities beyond traditional next-generation sequencing.
keyPoints: Technological progress suggests identification and isolation of single fetal cells as early as the first trimester.
Whole-gene genome amplification followed by copy number analysis is possible using arrays or next-generation sequencing.
Fetal gene expression profiling can be achieved through these advanced methods.

items.title: Clinical applications of liquid biopsy techniques
items.content: Liquid biopsies, including cfDNA profiling, have transformative potential in oncology, transplantation medicine, and obstetrics. They enable noninvasive diagnosis, risk assessment, and monitoring therapeutic responses across various medical fields.
keyPoints: cfDNA assays are used for early cancer detection, genotyping, response assessment, and MRD monitoring.
Liquid biopsy assays provide sensitive measures of tumor burden and can monitor anticancer therapy responses.
In transplantation medicine, rising levels of donor-derived cfDNA indicate acute allograft rejection.
Microbial cfDNA analysis is being developed for posttransplant infection screening and diagnosis.
